a: A Hex project is your workspace where you explore data, write code, and share results. It is a notebook and a shareable app rolled into one place, so the same work you do to analyze data can also be published for others to view.
fs: A project is the unit you build and ship in Hex: notebook on one side, app on the other.
fj: Thinking a project is just a static file or report, instead of a live, runnable workspace.

q: What is a cell in Hex?
a: A cell is one small building block inside a project that holds a single piece of work, like a SQL query, some Python, a chart, or text. You stack cells top to bottom to build up your analysis step by step.
fs: A cell is the smallest runnable block; a project is just an ordered stack of them.
fj: Cramming a whole analysis into one giant cell instead of splitting it into clear steps.

q: What does a SQL cell do in Hex?
a: A SQL cell lets you write a SQL query against your connected database without leaving the notebook. The rows it returns are automatically saved as a dataframe (a table of data) that later cells can use.
fs: A SQL cell pulls data and hands its result back as a named dataframe for everything downstream.
fj: Assuming SQL results vanish after the query, rather than becoming a dataframe you can reuse.

q: What is reactive execution in Hex?
a: Reactive execution means Hex watches how your cells depend on each other and reruns them in the right order for you. When you change one cell, only the cells that rely on it rerun automatically, so your results never go stale.
fs: Hex builds a dependency graph of cells and reruns just the affected downstream cells on any change.
fj: Expecting to manually rerun cells one by one, like in a plain Jupyter notebook.

q: What is the App Builder in Hex?
a: The App Builder is the tab where you turn your notebook into a clean, shareable app or dashboard. You pick which cells should be visible and drag them into a layout, hiding the raw code so stakeholders see only the polished result.
fs: App Builder is the publish layer: choose and arrange a subset of cells into a viewer-friendly app.
fj: Thinking you must rebuild your work from scratch to share it, instead of reusing the same cells.
